enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted comic book library manager that automates downloading, organizing, and renaming your collection, in the style of the *arr apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kapowarr is an open-source, self-hosted application for building and managing a comic book library, built to fit into the *arr suite of software alongside tools like Sonarr and Radarr.
It uses the ComicVine API to match volumes and issues, then searches configured download sources to automatically fetch, extract, rename, and file new comics into a folder structure you control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Kapowarr is aimed at home lab and self-hosted media enthusiasts who already run other *arr-style automation tools and want the same kind of hands-off library management for digital comics.

features.title: Automated volume monitoring
features.description: Monitor entire comic volumes and automatically search for and download new issues as they're released.

features.title: ComicVine metadata matching
features.description: Matches comics against the ComicVine database to pull accurate volume and issue metadata.

features.title: Multiple download sources
features.description: Supports direct download links, Pixeldrain, Mega, and other configured sources for fetching issues.

features.title: Automatic file organization
features.description: Extracts archives and renames/moves downloaded files into a folder structure and naming format you define.

features.title: Library import
features.description: Import an existing comic collection and match it against ComicVine to build a managed library.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Ships as a Docker image for straightforward self-hosted deployment alongside other *arr apps.

pros: Free and fully open source under GPL-3.0
Fits naturally into an existing *arr-based home server stack
Automates the tedious parts of comic collecting: matching, downloading, and renaming
Active Discord and subreddit for community support
cons: Requires self-hosting and some technical setup (Docker/manual install)
Relies on the ComicVine API and external download sources being available
No official mobile or desktop client — it's a web-based server app

faqs.question: Is Kapowarr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kapowarr is free and open source, released under the GPL-3.0 license. The developer accepts optional donations via Ko-Fi.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Kapowarr?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kapowarr is a self-hosted application, typically deployed via Docker on your own server or NAS.

faqs.question: Does Kapowarr download comics itself?
faqs.answer: Kapowarr automates searching and fetching from configured sources such as direct download links, Pixeldrain, and Mega, then organizes the resulting files for you.

faqs.question: What metadata source does Kapowarr use?
faqs.answer: Kapowarr integrates with the ComicVine API to match and pull metadata for volumes and issues.

use_cases.title: Automated comic collection management
use_cases.description: Automatically track and download new issues for monitored comic volumes.
use_cases.audience: Home lab and self-hosted media enthusiasts

use_cases.title: Organizing an existing comic archive
use_cases.description: Import a large existing folder of comics and have Kapowarr match and rename it consistently.
use_cases.audience: Comic collectors migrating to a managed library

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kapowarr is a free, open-source self-hosted app that automates comic book library management, downloading, and organizing in the *arr style.

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted digital library for reading and organizing EPUB, PDF, comics, and manga with dedicated readers and OPDS support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kavita is a self-hosted digital library platform for organizing and reading EPUB books, PDFs, comics, and manga privately on your own server, without relying on third-party cloud services.

It includes format-specific readers (including a dedicated webtoon mode and dual-page spreads), metadata management, full-text search, reading lists, and OPDS support so third-party e-reader apps can connect to your library.

long_form_blocks.heading: Kavita+
paragraphs: The core server is completely free and open source. Kavita+ is an optional paid add-on subscription that unlocks extras such as AniList synchronization and automatic metadata scraping.

features.title: Multi-format readers
features.description: Dedicated reading experiences for EPUB, PDF, comics, and manga, including a webtoon reading mode.

features.title: User management
features.description: Multi-user support with granular permissions and OIDC-based authentication.

features.title: Annotations
features.description: Highlight and annotate content, with export capabilities for notes.

features.title: Full-text search and metadata filtering
features.description: Search across your library content and filter by rich metadata fields.

features.title: OPDS support
features.description: Connect third-party OPDS-compatible reader apps to your Kavita library.

features.title: Automatic folder monitoring
features.description: Watches your library folders and updates automatically as files are added.

features.title: Send-to-Kindle
features.description: Send books directly to a Kindle device from within Kavita.

pros: Core server is free and fully open source
Broad format support across ebooks, comics, and manga in one app
Actively developed with community-driven feature requests
OPDS support enables use with many third-party reader apps
cons: Requires self-hosting your own server
Some advanced metadata and sync features require the paid Kavita+ add-on
No dedicated first-party native mobile app is highlighted on the site; mobile access relies on OPDS clients or the web UI

faqs.question: Is Kavita free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Kavita server is free and open source. An optional Kavita+ subscription adds extras like AniList sync and automatic metadata, starting at a discounted first month before renewing at a monthly rate.

faqs.question: What file formats does Kavita support?
faqs.answer: Kavita supports EPUB and PDF ebooks as well as common comic and manga archive formats.

faqs.question: Can I use other reading apps with Kavita?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kavita exposes an OPDS feed so it can be used with compatible third-party reader apps.

faqs.question: Do I need my own server to run Kavita?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kavita is self-hosted software that you install and run on your own server or NAS.

use_cases.title: Personal ebook and comic library
use_cases.description: Centralize EPUB, PDF, comic, and manga collections in one self-hosted reader.

use_cases.title: Manga and webtoon reading
use_cases.description: Read manga and webtoons with a purpose-built reading mode.

pricing_plans.price: From $2 first month, then billed monthly

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kavita is a free, self-hosted digital library for EPUB, PDF, comics, and manga, with dedicated readers, OPDS support, and an optional Kavita+ add-on.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted media server for organizing and reading comics, manga, graphic novels, and eBooks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Komga is a free, open-source media server for organizing and reading comics, manga, graphic novels, magazines, and eBooks from your own storage.
It organizes content into libraries, collections, and reading lists, and includes an integrated web-based reader along with metadata editing tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Komga suits self-hosters who want a centralized, private way to browse and read a personal comic or ebook collection across devices, including through third-party client apps.

features.title: Library and collection organization
features.description: Group content into libraries, collections, and custom reading lists.

features.title: Multi-format support
features.description: Reads CBZ, CBR, PDF, and EPUB files.

features.title: Integrated web reader
features.description: Read directly in the browser without a separate client app.

features.title: Metadata editing
features.description: Edit and manage series and book metadata within the app.

features.title: Third-party client support
features.description: Compatible with OPDS readers and the Mihon (formerly Tachiyomi) extension.

features.title: Multi-language interface
features.description: Available in several languages including English, French, Spanish, and Chinese.

pros: Completely free and open source
Clean, integrated web reader with no extra client required
Broad format support for both comics and ebooks
Active community with Discord and translation support via Crowdin
cons: Requires self-hosting and basic server administration
No official mobile app of its own — mobile access relies on OPDS or Mihon-compatible clients

faqs.question: Is Komga free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Komga is free and open-source software available on GitHub.

faqs.question: What formats does Komga support?
faqs.answer: Komga supports CBZ, CBR, PDF, and EPUB files.

faqs.question: Can I read Komga on mobile?
faqs.answer: Komga's web reader works in mobile browsers, and it supports third-party OPDS and Mihon-compatible reader apps for a native mobile reading experience.

faqs.question: Does Komga need to be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Komga runs on your own server or NAS as a self-hosted application.

use_cases.title: Personal comic and manga library
use_cases.description: Organize and read a personal collection of comics and manga from any device.

use_cases.title: eBook server
use_cases.description: Serve EPUB and PDF books alongside comics in one unified library.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Komga is a free, open-source self-hosted server for organizing and reading comics, manga, graphic novels, and eBooks.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, self-hosted comics, manga, and digital book server with OPDS support, built with Rust and React.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Stump is a free and open-source, self-hosted server for comics, manga, and digital books, built with Rust and React. It supports EPUB, PDF, CBZ/ZIP, and CBR/RAR formats with built-in readers for each.
The project is under active development but is still officially in beta, ahead of a planned stable 1.0 release, maintained as a hobby project by Aaron Leopold.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Stump appeals to self-hosters who want a lightweight, OPDS-compatible reading server with multi-user support and e-reader syncing, and who are comfortable running beta software.

features.title: OPDS v1.2 and v2.0 support
features.description: Connect e-readers and third-party OPDS clients directly to your Stump library.

features.title: Multi-format readers
features.description: Built-in readers for EPUB, PDF, CBZ/ZIP, and CBR/RAR files.

features.title: Multi-user accounts
features.description: Permissions and age-based access controls for multiple users.

features.title: Annotations and highlights
features.description: Highlight and annotate EPUB books within the reader.

features.title: Kobo and KoReader sync
features.description: Sync reading progress with Kobo devices and KoReader.

features.title: OIDC authentication
features.description: Supports OpenID Connect for single sign-on.

features.title: Multiple client apps
features.description: Available as a Docker deployment, pre-built binaries, a Tauri desktop app, and a React Native mobile app.

pros: Free and open source (MIT licensed core)
Lightweight Rust backend with a modern React interface
Good OPDS and e-reader device sync support
Available across Docker, desktop, and mobile clients
cons: Still in beta ahead of a 1.0 release, so stability and features can change
Maintained as a hobby project with no guaranteed development timeline
Requires self-hosting and technical setup

faqs.question: Is Stump free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stump is free and open source, licensed under MIT (with GPL-3.0 for its mobile app).

faqs.question: Is Stump production-ready?
faqs.answer: Stump is under active development but is still considered beta software; it has not yet reached a stable 1.0 release.

faqs.question: Does Stump support e-readers?
faqs.answer: Yes, Stump supports OPDS v1.2/v2.0 and syncs reading progress with Kobo devices and KoReader.

faqs.question: What platforms is Stump available on?
faqs.answer: Stump can be deployed via Docker or pre-built binaries, and also offers a Tauri-based desktop app and a React Native mobile app.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted comic and manga server
use_cases.description: Host and read a personal comics/manga collection with multi-user access.

use_cases.title: E-reader syncing
use_cases.description: Keep reading progress synced across Kobo and KoReader devices.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Stump is a free, open-source self-hosted server for comics, manga, and digital books with OPDS, e-reader sync, and desktop/mobile clients.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source digital repository platform used by academic, non-profit, and commercial organizations to preserve and share digital content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DSpace is an open-source software platform for building and managing digital repositories, used to host, preserve, and share digital content such as PDFs, images, and audio/video files.

It is distributed under a BSD open-source license with no licensing fees, and is developed and supported by its user community with the help of Lyrasis. DSpace is available in 22 languages and includes granular, group-based access controls.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: DSpace is widely used by research libraries and institutions to run institutional repositories for publications, datasets, theses, and other scholarly or organizational content.

features.title: Digital content preservation
features.description: Manages and preserves many file formats, from PDFs and images to audio and video.

features.title: Group-based access controls
features.description: Granular permissions for managing who can view or manage repository content.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Available in 22 languages for international institutions.

features.title: External system integrations
features.description: Integrates with services such as ORCID, OpenAIRE, and ROR.

features.title: DSpace-CRIS
features.description: A merged platform extension supporting research information management use cases.

pros: Completely free with no licensing costs
Mature project with a large, active community and Lyrasis-backed governance
Used by thousands of institutions worldwide
Strong integrations with scholarly identifier and metadata services
cons: Requires self-hosting and dedicated IT/systems administration to run and maintain
Setup and customization can require developer expertise
No official managed hosting from the core project itself (relies on institutional or third-party hosting)

faqs.question: Is DSpace free?
faqs.answer: Yes, DSpace is free and open source under a BSD license, with no subscription fees.

faqs.question: Who maintains DSpace?
faqs.answer: DSpace is developed and supported by its user community, with organizational support from Lyrasis.

faqs.question: What is DSpace used for?
faqs.answer: DSpace is used to build institutional and subject-based repositories for research publications, datasets, and other digital content, mainly by academic and non-profit organizations.

faqs.question: Does DSpace support open access compliance?
faqs.answer: DSpace integrates with services like ORCID, OpenAIRE, and ROR, which are commonly used to support open access and research reporting requirements.

use_cases.title: Institutional repository
use_cases.description: Host and preserve a university or organization's research output and publications.

use_cases.title: Dataset and media repository
use_cases.description: Manage subject-based, dataset, or media collections with access controls.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: DSpace is a free, open-source digital repository platform for preserving and sharing research and institutional content.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source digital repository platform for institutions to manage and share research publications, theses, and other outputs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: EPrints is an open-source digital repository platform used by research institutions to create repositories for publications, theses, and grey literature, supporting open access compliance.

It has been developed for over two decades and is maintained by EPrints Services at the University of Southampton, which also offers optional commercial hosting, storage, and support services alongside the free software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: EPrints is aimed at universities and research organizations, particularly in the UK, that need to run an institutional repository and meet funder open access requirements.

features.title: Research output repository
features.description: Store and showcase publications, theses, and grey literature in a customizable repository.

features.title: Open access compliance support
features.description: Helps institutions meet research funder open access requirements.

features.title: Usage analytics and reporting
features.description: Built-in tools for tracking repository usage and generating reports.

features.title: Institutional system integration
features.description: Integrates with existing institutional infrastructure and systems.

features.title: Customizable design
features.description: Repository presentation can be tailored for specialized research communities.

pros: Free, open-source core with over two decades of development
Optional professional hosting and support available from EPrints Services
Proven track record with UK universities and other research institutions
Designed specifically around open access and funder compliance needs
cons: Self-hosted deployment requires systems administration expertise
Commercial hosting, storage, and support are paid add-ons, not included free
Primarily oriented toward academic/research use cases rather than general-purpose content management

faqs.question: Is EPrints free?
faqs.answer: Yes, EPrints is free, open-source software. Optional commercial hosting, storage, and support are available for a fee through EPrints Services.

faqs.question: Who develops EPrints?
faqs.answer: EPrints is maintained by EPrints Services at the University of Southampton.

faqs.question: What is EPrints used for?
faqs.answer: EPrints is used by universities and research organizations to build institutional repositories for publications, theses, and other research outputs, and to support open access compliance.

faqs.question: Does EPrints offer managed hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, EPrints Services offers optional paid hosting, storage, and support alongside the free open-source software.

use_cases.title: University institutional repository
use_cases.description: Host and manage a university's research publications and theses.

use_cases.title: Open access compliance
use_cases.description: Meet funder open access mandates by publishing research outputs in a compliant repository.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: EPrints is an open-source digital repository platform for universities and research institutions to manage publications and support open access.

enrichment.short_description: Modular, open-source repository system for managing and disseminating digital content in libraries, archives, and cultural institutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Fedora (Flexible Extensible Digital Object Repository Architecture) is a robust, modular, open-source repository system for managing and disseminating digital content, used by digital libraries, archives, and organizations with large collections of cultural, historic, and scientific materials.
The project is maintained by Lyrasis as its organizational home, with a dedicated program team, active mailing lists, and a community Slack channel supporting adopters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Fedora is used by universities, national libraries, cultural heritage organizations, and government agencies that need a flexible backend repository for complex digital collections, often as a component within larger platforms like Islandora or Samvera Hyrax.

features.title: Modular architecture
features.description: Flexible, component-based design that can be adapted to different repository needs.

features.title: Complex digital object support
features.description: Built to manage large, diverse collections of digital content types.

features.title: Specialized access controls
features.description: Supports fine-grained access policies for managed content.

features.title: Ecosystem integration
features.description: Commonly used as the storage/preservation layer beneath platforms such as Islandora and Samvera Hyrax.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing costs
Backed by Lyrasis with an active community and governance structure
Proven at scale across major libraries, archives, and cultural institutions
Serves as a flexible foundation used by other repository platforms
cons: Requires significant technical expertise to deploy and maintain
Typically used as infrastructure within a larger application rather than as a standalone end-user tool
Legacy Fedora 3 and current versions coexist, which can add complexity for new adopters

faqs.question: Is Fedora Commons Repository free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Fedora is free and open-source software with no licensing costs.

faqs.question: Who maintains Fedora?
faqs.answer: Fedora is maintained by Lyrasis, which serves as its organizational home, along with an active community of contributors.

faqs.question: Is Fedora a standalone application?
faqs.answer: Fedora is typically used as a repository backend, often underlying front-end platforms such as Islandora or Samvera Hyrax, rather than as a standalone consumer application.

faqs.question: Who uses Fedora?
faqs.answer: Fedora is used by academic institutions, national libraries, cultural heritage organizations, and government agencies worldwide.

use_cases.title: Digital library backend
use_cases.description: Provide the preservation and access layer for a digital library or archive.

use_cases.title: Foundation for repository platforms
use_cases.description: Serve as the underlying repository system for platforms like Islandora and Samvera Hyrax.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fedora is a free, open-source, modular repository system used by libraries, archives, and cultural institutions to manage digital content.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source, turn-key research data management repository built by CERN and partners for institutions to manage and share research data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: InvenioRDM is a turn-key research data management repository built to help organizations store, organize, and share research datasets and related materials.

It is free and open source, led by CERN with contributions from Northwestern University and the broader community, and includes a REST API, DOI registration, customizable metadata and vocabularies, and community features for managing submissions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: InvenioRDM is built for research institutions, universities, and scientific organizations that need a scalable, standards-based repository to manage and publish research data with DOIs and rich metadata.

features.title: Customizable metadata and workflows
features.description: Adapt metadata schemas and submission workflows to institutional needs.

features.title: DOI registration
features.description: Register DOIs for published datasets and research outputs.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Extensive API access for integration with other research systems.

features.title: Search and discovery
features.description: Powerful search across deposited research data and metadata.

features.title: Community features
features.description: Organize submissions and curation workflows around communities within the repository.

features.title: Interoperability
features.description: Built to interoperate with external systems and standards used in research data management.

pros: Free and open source, led by CERN with active institutional backing
Highly scalable architecture proven in large research contexts
Strong metadata customization and REST API for integrations
Modern, actively maintained with regular versioned releases
cons: Requires self-hosting and technical resources to deploy and operate
Complex feature set that may be more than smaller institutions need
No official managed SaaS hosting offered directly by the core project

faqs.question: Is InvenioRDM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, InvenioRDM is free and open-source software available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who develops InvenioRDM?
faqs.answer: InvenioRDM is led by CERN, with contributions from Northwestern University and the broader open-source community.

faqs.question: What is InvenioRDM used for?
faqs.answer: InvenioRDM is used by institutions to manage and share research data, register DOIs, and provide standards-based discovery and access.

faqs.question: Can I try InvenioRDM before installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, the project maintains a public demo site where you can explore its features before deploying your own instance.

use_cases.title: Research data repository
use_cases.description: Store, organize, and publish research datasets with DOIs and rich metadata.

use_cases.title: Institutional data management
use_cases.description: Provide a standards-compliant platform for managing research outputs across departments.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: InvenioRDM is a free, open-source research data management repository led by CERN, with DOI registration, custom metadata, and a REST API.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source digital asset management platform, built on Drupal, for managing and preserving digital collections in libraries, archives, and museums.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Islandora is an open-source digital asset management platform for the collaborative authoring, management, discovery, and stewardship of digital collections, built on top of the Drupal CMS.

It integrates Apache Solr for indexing and discovery, supports IIIF for large-scale image access, and can optionally connect to a Fedora Commons repository for preservation and linked-data capabilities. It is maintained by the Islandora Foundation and an international community of contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Islandora is used across government, library, archive, and museum sectors by institutions that need a customizable, Drupal-based platform for managing and preserving digital collections.

features.title: Drupal-based content management
features.description: Built on Drupal CMS, giving institutions a flexible, extensible content management foundation.

features.title: Apache Solr integration
features.description: Enterprise-level indexing and search for large digital collections.

features.title: Optional Fedora Commons integration
features.description: Connects to a Fedora repository for preservation and linked-data support.

features.title: IIIF support
features.description: Enables large-scale, standards-based access to digital images.

features.title: Sandbox environment
features.description: A demo environment with sample content for evaluating the platform before deployment.

pros: Free to use and open source, with a permanent commitment to remain so
Built on the widely-used Drupal ecosystem, giving access to a large module and developer community
Backed by the Islandora Foundation with active governance and community groups
Strong support for standards like IIIF for image-heavy digital collections
cons: Requires Drupal and systems administration expertise to deploy and maintain
Optional Fedora integration adds architectural complexity for full preservation features
Best suited to institutions with dedicated technical staff rather than casual self-hosters

faqs.question: Is Islandora free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Islandora is released under open-source licenses and the project states it is free to use and always will be.

faqs.question: What is Islandora built on?
faqs.answer: Islandora is built on the Drupal CMS, with Apache Solr for search and optional Fedora Commons integration for preservation.

faqs.question: Who maintains Islandora?
faqs.answer: Islandora is maintained by the Islandora Foundation and an international community of contributors, with committees and interest groups guiding development.

faqs.question: Who uses Islandora?
faqs.answer: Islandora is used by government agencies, libraries, archives, and museums worldwide to manage digital collections.

use_cases.title: Digital collections repository
use_cases.description: Manage and provide access to large digital collections of images, documents, and other objects.

use_cases.title: Preservation with Fedora
use_cases.description: Pair Islandora's front end with a Fedora Commons backend for long-term digital preservation.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Islandora is a free, open-source, Drupal-based digital asset management platform for libraries, archives, and museums.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Ruby on Rails repository engine from the Samvera community for building customizable institutional digital repositories.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hyrax is a Ruby on Rails engine built by the Samvera community that provides the foundation for developing digital repository applications. Rather than a standalone application, it's a pluggable engine that institutions mount inside their own Rails app to build a customized repository.

Hyrax supports custom repository object types, configurable deposit workflows, and flexible metadata schemas, with features that can be selectively enabled through an administrative interface. It is licensed under Apache 2.0, making it free for both institutional and commercial use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Hyrax is aimed at institutions and developer teams within the Samvera community who need a flexible, code-level starting point for a custom digital repository rather than an out-of-the-box application.

features.title: Rails engine architecture
features.description: Mounts as an engine inside a host Rails application, giving developers full control over customization.

features.title: Custom object types
features.description: Define custom repository object types to match institutional content models.

features.title: Configurable deposit workflows
features.description: Set up custom review and approval workflows for content deposit.

features.title: Flexible metadata schemas
features.description: Adapt metadata models to institutional or domain-specific requirements.

features.title: Selective feature toggles
features.description: Enable or disable features through an administrative interface as needed.

pros: Free and open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
Highly customizable via its Rails engine architecture
Backed by the Samvera community with 30+ institutional partners
Suitable for both commercial and institutional use
cons: Requires Ruby on Rails development expertise to implement — not a turn-key app
Typically depends on other Samvera ecosystem components (e.g. Fedora, Solr, Blacklight) for a full deployment
Best suited to institutions with dedicated developer resources rather than casual self-hosters

faqs.question: Is Samvera Hyrax free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hyrax is free and open source, licensed under Apache 2.0.

faqs.question: Is Hyrax a ready-to-use application?
faqs.answer: No — Hyrax is a Ruby on Rails engine that developers mount within their own Rails application to build a customized repository, rather than a standalone installable app.

faqs.question: Who develops Hyrax?
faqs.answer: Hyrax is developed by the Samvera community, a collaborative network of institutional partners building shared repository software.

faqs.question: What is Hyrax used for?
faqs.answer: Hyrax is used to build institutional, data, or digital object repositories with custom workflows and metadata.

use_cases.title: Custom institutional repository
use_cases.description: Build a tailored digital repository application on top of the Hyrax Rails engine.

use_cases.title: Research data and digital object management
use_cases.description: Implement configurable deposit workflows for varied content types.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Samvera Hyrax is a free, open-source Ruby on Rails engine for building customizable institutional digital repositories.
